GDL-01.title: Medical Consultation Required
GDL-01.snippet: Antibiotics should only be taken when prescribed by a certified health professional. Using antibiotics without medical guidance increases the risk of inappropriate use and contributes to antimicrobial resistance.

GDL-02.title: Complete the Prescribed Course
GDL-02.snippet: Stopping an antibiotic course before completion, even if symptoms improve, can allow partially resistant bacteria to survive and multiply, accelerating antimicrobial resistance.

GDL-03.title: Avoid Self-Medication
GDL-03.snippet: Using leftover antibiotics or self-medicating without a prescription is a major driver of AMR. The wrong antibiotic or wrong duration can select for resistant bacteria.

GDL-04.title: Antibiotics vs Viral Infections
GDL-04.snippet: Antibiotics do not work against viral infections such as common colds and most coughs. Using antibiotics for viral symptoms provides no benefit and increases AMR risk.

GDL-05.title: Dose Adherence Matters
GDL-05.snippet: Skipping doses creates periods of sub-therapeutic drug levels, giving bacteria an opportunity to develop resistance mechanisms.

GDL-06.title: Frequent Antibiotic Use
GDL-06.snippet: Frequent antibiotic use within short periods increases selective pressure on bacteria, raising the likelihood of resistance development.

GDL-07.title: Never Share Antibiotics
GDL-07.snippet: Sharing antibiotics with family members or friends means the wrong drug for the wrong infection. This dangerous practice promotes the spread of resistant bacteria.
